title: Dream big and never give up: Dr Robert Langer | Biomedical Scientist

# Dream big and never give up: Dr Robert Langer

Open-access content John Windell — Thu 4 Sep 2025

**An exclusive interview with Dr Robert Langer, one of the most cited scientists in any field, ever, a founder of Moderna and holder of more than a thousand patents around the globe.**

Image credit | Getty

**At a glance**

* Robert Langer is a scientist and professor at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, where he leads the Langer Lab.
* Langer was a founder of biotech firm Moderna, has over a thousand worldwide patents and an estimated net worth of $1.2bn.
* He is estimated to be the most cited engineer in history, with more than 415,000 citations and more than 1500 articles published.

* * *

Dr Robert Langer is a giant of modern biomedical science. Though his career stretches back to the 1970s, he has still accomplished more than might be reasonably expected from any scientist over the course of those 50 years.

His start was not especially auspicious, with an undergraduate degree in chemical engineering at Cornell University and then his Sc.D. from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) in the same subject. But his next move was a little more unusual for the time, choosing not to pursue the traditional route of taking up a lucrative position in industry but opting instead to work in a hospital – the Harvard Medical School in Boston.

At Harvard, Langer’s aptitude and focus soon settled on researching and perfecting forms of drug delivery. This early work culminated in the development of a synthetic polymer system, which has since been the basis of much of the technology for the safe and effective administration of various therapies for different forms of cancer.

Many other innovations have since followed, such as tissue engineering and organ-on-a-chip technology, as have the plaudits and prizes. His awards and honours, beginning with the Gairdner International Award in 1996, now number in the hundreds. They include the 2006 US National Medal of Science and 2013 National Medal of Technology and Innovation National Medal, and the 2015 Queen Elizabeth Prize for Engineering for his work on controlled-release large molecule drug delivery.

Today, he heads the MIT Langer Lab, which works “at the interface of biotechnology and materials science”. According to Google Scholar, he has a jaw-dropping 450,000-plus citations. He also has over a thousand worldwide patents, many of which have been licensed to companies working in the pharmaceutical, biotech and medical device sectors. In 2010 he was one of the founders of Moderna, which became one of the leading manufacturers of the mRNA vaccines during the COVID pandemic.

As a consequence of his scientific prowess and business nous, these ventures have, together, made him very rich. In 2024, _Forbes_ magazine put his net worth at $1.2bn, not that it’s apparent during the Zoom call for this interview, with Langer relaxing in his sweat pants and top, sipping on a can of diet cola.

> I’d rather do something that will hopefully have a giant impact on the world. I’d rather take a risk
> 
> 

**When you meet people who are not familiar with your work, how do you explain what you do?**

I say I teach and I do research. I’d probably say I do what I’d call biomedical engineering. Some examples of that are new drug delivery systems, and I might give the example of the nanoparticle work that Moderna and others use. That’s a good example of why drug delivery is important. If you try to give that [mRNA] vaccine without the nanoparticles, it gets destroyed. With the nanoparticles, it works and it saved many millions of lives. I’d say the other area we work on is trying to create new tissues and organs from scratch, which has led to artificial skin for burn victims, and new blood vessels for, say, wounded Ukrainian soldiers. Maybe also creating tissues and organs on a chip, which might someday reduce or eliminate testing in animals or humans.

It’s not the answer people look for, but I also say what singles [Langer Lab] out more than anything are the students and postdocs who have trained under us, around 7000 people, and they’ve done remarkably well. To give you one example in England, Molly Stevens was one of my postdocs over 20 years ago. Now she is a professor at Oxford and was made a dame a year ago. I think we have 12 people who are professors at Harvard, 11 at Stanford, and 10 at MIT.

**What work do you have going on in Langer Lab today that excites you and that we might be hearing about?**

We’re doing a lot of work with the Gates Foundation on new drug delivery systems for the developing world, both in medicine and nutrition. We’re developing things such as vaccines that might need just one injection and pills that might last for a month, because one of the issues is people don’t come back a second time. And we’re developing ways of tackling malnutrition and protecting vital nutrients. So you can put them in things like bouillon... Some of those are going to be introduced very soon.

We’re also developing pills that can deliver things like insulin or mRNA. I mentioned organs on a chip earlier ‑ we’re also developing brains on a chip, and that might enable us to study diseases like Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s, and maybe make new treatments for them and get drugs across the blood-brain barrier.

**You’ve enjoyed a long and very successful career. Can you pinpoint a moment or a decision that set you on that trajectory?**

When I finished my degree in 1974, a lot of my classmates went to work for oil companies. That was a standard thing, still is. But I didn’t do that. I went to work for a hospital. It was by far the lowest paying job, and a lot of people thought it was not a smart thing to do. But I was the only engineer in the surgery department. That decision changed my life. I was a chemical engineer, and I thought differently about how to address issues. I don’t want to give myself that much credit for it, but I was just looking for something where I could help people. I wasn’t concerned about money. In the end I did pretty well! But the thing is, everything flowed from that decision. I worked on projects like isolating the first blood vessel inhibitor, angiogenesis inhibitors, and developing nano and micro-particles that could deliver large molecules. All that came because I was fortunate enough to be in the hospital and to have worked with Judah Folkman. He was a visionary surgeon and a very good role model for me.

Image credit | Alamy

**The list of prizes and awards you’ve received is long and impressive. How important are those accolades?**

They’ve been important. I think it shows that engineering is important, that areas such as drug delivery are important. If I were to pick some that stand out, those I’ve got from the president of the United States, so Barack Obama and George W Bush. The Queen Elizabeth Prize was very special. It was a great award recognising engineering. I also really appreciated Queen Elizabeth and her family spending so much time with us. I was able to bring all three of my children, my wife and my sister, and that was nice! If a paper gets accepted or we get an award, it makes me feel very happy. I’ve had, like I say, students get great jobs and have good things happen to them, and to this day get a thrill out of that.

**Looking at biotech in general, what do you think have been the big breakthroughs in recent years?**

I think the mRNA work has been a big breakthrough. There are many aspects to that, including the delivery systems. I think gene editing has been a breakthrough, the CRISPR work. I’d say some of these cellular therapies, which includes regenerative medicine and tissue engineering that we’ve been involved in. There’ve been a number of examples like that. I think sequencing the human genome, to me that’s an achievement. Those are some examples.

**AI has had a big impact on research in recent years, from number crunching to identifying new targets for drugs. Has it affected your work?**

To me, AI isn’t new. Certainly ChatGPT has been a tremendous demonstration of AI that has all of a sudden made it newsworthy. I don’t know exactly where machine learning stops and AI starts. I started a company in 1999, Transform, where we used machine learning, or artificial intelligence, to help us. We had a lot of data! One of the challenges in drug delivery was making drugs more soluble. Basically we looked at lots of different excipient molecules. You could add substances, you could add in combinations. And we would iterate, we’d make a thousand, and then pick the best ones and analyse their structures by machine learning and then use that for the next thousand. So we’ve been using machine learning on our work for a long while and we continue to use it. So it’s not new, but I do feel it’s accelerating.

**With science advancing so quickly now, where do you think the frontiers of biotech research will be heading?**

That’s a great question. I think one of them will be artificial intelligence for helping design better drugs, better antibodies, better cells and so forth. I feel like I’m a giant believer in basic research. Right now, I feel the ones that have names are things we’ve talked about, such as gene editing, mRNA, cell therapies, including making new tissues and organs. But the things that might end up being most important might not even have a name yet. They haven’t been invented or the key papers haven’t been written. I mentioned CRISPR earlier, the key paper for that was written in 2012, so if you asked somebody in 2011, well, gene editing might have been the last thing they’d say. So basic research, I think, will continue to lead to really important discoveries.

Image credit | Alamy

**How do you think science should face current challenges, such as anti-vax sentiment?**

I think you just do the best you can. When the media ask me to speak to them, I almost always do that because I feel like whatever I can reach in a lecture, I can reach a hundred thousand times more.

**For young bio-research scientists just starting out on their careers, do you have any key advice about what they should and shouldn’t do?**

A lot of people will do what I’ll call incremental research. It’s a sure thing. You can get research papers, you can get grants. But I’d rather do something that will hopefully have a giant impact on the world. I’d rather take a risk. If you do that, you’re going to run into obstacles, and you don’t want to give up easily. So it is important to try to raise substantial funds. Funds give you flexibility. One of my friends went to one of my commencement speeches and she said: “Bob, your talk is like ‘dream big and never give up’.”

**Looking ahead for the next 10, 20 years, is there anything that you would still like to achieve? How would you like to see it going?**

I guess I’d like to come up with more ideas. I hope I can help drive them into the clinic and make new treatments for people. And I hope I’ll be able to train more future leaders and help them on what they’re doing.

**Fifty years into your career do you still feel that enthusiasm and drive for the work?**

I do. I still get a tremendous amount of satisfaction out of it. I’m still working all the time, even when I’m not supposed to be! The way I think about it is that I have a limited set of skills. I mean, I’m not a politician, I’m not a molecular biologist. But with my skills, I ask what can I do that will have the biggest positive impact on the world? I don’t know that when I was younger I thought about it that way, but that’s how I think about it now.

* * *

**Explore further**

* Website of the Langer Lab at MIT: [b.link/evvycvsr](https://www.b.link/evvycvsr)
* Robert Langer’s page on Google Scholar: [b.link/cdpo81wc](https://www.b.link/cdpo81wc)
* Robert Langer on human origins and the future of humanity: [b.link/3rc8gzj9](https://www.b.link/3rc8gzj9)

* * *
